info.name: Laravel API
info.description:
# Laravel API тАФ Complete Reference

## Overview

The **Laravel API** is a RESTful web service built on the [Laravel PHP framework](https://laravel.com/), one of the most popular and expressive PHP frameworks in the world. Laravel provides an elegant syntax, a powerful ORM (Eloquent), built-in validation, routing, and a rich ecosystem of tools that make building APIs fast, reliable, and maintainable.

This API exposes structured application data over HTTP in JSON format. It follows REST (Representational State Transfer) architectural principles:

- Each **URL** identifies a unique resource or collection of resources (e.g., `/api/categories` represents all categories).
- **HTTP methods** define the action: `GET` retrieves data, `POST` creates a new resource, `PUT`/`PATCH` updates an existing resource, `DELETE` removes a resource.
- **Responses are stateless**: every request is self-contained and includes all the information the server needs to process it. No session state is maintained between requests.
- **Responses are self-describing**: the HTTP status code, response headers, and JSON body together fully communicate the outcome of every request.

This collection documents every available endpoint in the Laravel API, including required headers, full request body schemas, complete response structures with field-by-field explanations, HTTP status code semantics, and practical usage guidelines. Whether you are building a front-end application, a mobile client, or integrating with another back-end service, this reference gives you everything you need.

The API is currently configured for **local development** and runs on your machine at `http://127.0.0.1:8000`. All API endpoints are prefixed with `/api/` to namespace them away from any web UI routes that Laravel may also serve.

---

## Base URL

```
http://127.0.0.1:8000
```

All endpoint paths in this collection are relative to this base URL. For example, the full URL for the categories endpoint is:

```
http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories
```

To avoid hardcoding this value in every request, create a Postman environment variable named `base_url` with the value `http://127.0.0.1:8000`, then reference it in all request URLs as `{{base_url}}/api/...`.

**How to set up the environment variable in Postman:**
1. Click **Environments** in the left sidebar.
2. Click **+** to create a new environment. Name it `Laravel Local`.
3. Add a variable: Variable = `base_url`, Initial Value = `http://127.0.0.1:8000`, Current Value = `http://127.0.0.1:8000`.
4. Click **Save**.
5. Select `Laravel Local` from the environment dropdown in the top-right corner of Postman.
6. Update all request URLs to use `{{base_url}}/api/categories` etc.

---

## Available Endpoints

| Method | Path | Description | Auth Required |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `GET` | `/api/categories` | Retrieve a list of all categories | No |
| `POST` | `/api/categories` | Create a new category | No |

> More endpoints will be added as the API grows. Each new endpoint will be documented in this collection with the same level of detail.

---

## Required Request Headers

Every request to this API must include the correct HTTP headers. Omitting them may result in unexpected responses, including HTML error pages instead of JSON.

### `Accept: application/json`

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Header name** | `Accept` |
| **Header value** | `application/json` |
| **Required for** | All requests (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) |

The `Accept` header tells the server what media type the client can understand. When set to `application/json`, Laravel's exception handler and response layer will return all responses тАФ including validation errors and server errors тАФ as JSON objects rather than HTML pages.

Without this header:
- A `404 Not Found` will return a full Laravel HTML error page.
- A `422 Unprocessable Entity` (validation failure) will return an HTML page instead of a structured JSON error with field-level messages.
- A `500 Internal Server Error` will return an HTML debug page (in debug mode) or a generic HTML page (in production mode).

**How to add it in Postman:**
1. Open the request.
2. Click the **Headers** tab.
3. Add a new row: Key = `Accept`, Value = `application/json`.
4. Ensure the checkbox on the left is **checked** (enabled).

### `Content-Type: application/json`

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Header name** | `Content-Type` |
| **Header value** | `application/json` |
| **Required for** | `POST`, `PUT`, and `PATCH` requests that send a JSON body |

The `Content-Type` header tells the server the format of the data you are sending in the request body. For `GET` requests, this header is not needed because there is no request body. For `POST /api/categories`, you must include this header so Laravel knows to parse the body as JSON.

Without this header on a POST request, Laravel will not parse the JSON body, and `$request->input('name')` will return `null`, causing a validation error.

**How to add it in Postman:**
- When you select **Body тЖТ raw тЖТ JSON** in Postman, the `Content-Type: application/json` header is added automatically. You do not need to add it manually in that case.

---

## JSON Response Structure

All successful responses from this API return JSON. The structure depends on the endpoint, but all category resources share a common set of fields.

### Category Object

A **category** is the primary resource exposed by this API. Each category object returned by the API has the following fields:

| Field | Type | Nullable | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `id` | `integer` | No | The unique numeric identifier for this category. Auto-assigned by the database. Immutable after creation. |
| `name` | `string` | No | The human-readable display name of the category. UTF-8 encoded. |
| `description` | `string` | Yes | An optional longer description of the category. May be `null` if not provided at creation time. |
| `created_at` | `string` (ISO 8601) | No | The UTC timestamp when this category was created. Format: `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS.000000Z`. |
| `updated_at` | `string` (ISO 8601) | No | The UTC timestamp when this category was last updated. Equals `created_at` if never updated. Format: `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS.000000Z`. |

### Field Details

#### `id` (integer)

- **Type:** Positive integer (e.g., `1`, `42`, `1000`)
- **Source:** Auto-incremented primary key, assigned by the database engine at creation time
- **Immutable:** Yes тАФ this value is set once and never changes, even if the category is updated
- **Unique:** Guaranteed to be unique across all category records
- **Never zero or negative** in a real database
- **Primary use:** Reference this value when building URLs for single-resource endpoints:
  ```
  GET    /api/categories/1
  PUT    /api/categories/1
  DELETE /api/categories/1
  ```
- **In your front-end:** Store this as the key in dropdowns, lists, and state management so you can identify which category the user selected

#### `name` (string)

- **Type:** UTF-8 string
- **Source:** Provided by the client in the request body when creating or updating a category
- **Required at creation:** Yes тАФ the API will return `422 Unprocessable Entity` if `name` is missing or empty
- **Mutable:** Yes тАФ can be changed via `PUT` or `PATCH` endpoints
- **Uniqueness:** Not guaranteed at the API level unless enforced by the database schema
- **May contain:** Spaces, punctuation, accented characters, Unicode (e.g., `"├Йlectronique"`, `"B├╝cher"`, `"╨Ъ╨░╤В╨╡╨│╨╛╤А╤Ц╤П"`)
- **Primary use:** Display to end users in navigation menus, filter dropdowns, product listings, and category selectors

#### `description` (string, nullable)

- **Type:** UTF-8 string or `null`
- **Source:** Optionally provided by the client in the request body
- **Required at creation:** No тАФ if omitted, the field will be `null` in the response
- **Mutable:** Yes тАФ can be set or updated via `PUT` or `PATCH` endpoints
- **May contain:** Long-form text, line breaks, HTML (if your application renders it), Unicode
- **Primary use:** Provide additional context about a category тАФ for example, shown on a category detail page or used as a tooltip
- **Null handling:** Always check for `null` before rendering this field in your UI:
  ```javascript
  const desc = category.description ?? 'No description provided.';
  ```

#### `created_at` (string, ISO 8601)

- **Type:** UTC timestamp string in ISO 8601 format
- **Example:** `"2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z"`
- **Source:** Set automatically by Laravel's Eloquent ORM when the record is created. You cannot set this manually.
- **Immutable:** Yes тАФ never changes after creation
- **Use case:** Display creation date in the UI, sort records by creation time, audit trails
- **Parsing in JavaScript:**
  ```javascript
  const date = new Date(category.created_at);
  console.log(date.toLocaleDateString()); // e.g. "1/15/2024"
  ```

#### `updated_at` (string, ISO 8601)

- **Type:** UTC timestamp string in ISO 8601 format
- **Example:** `"2024-03-22T14:45:00.000000Z"`
- **Source:** Set automatically by Laravel's Eloquent ORM. Equals `created_at` when the record has never been updated.
- **Mutable:** Yes тАФ updated automatically by Laravel every time the record is saved
- **Use case:** Show "last modified" timestamps, detect stale cache entries, implement optimistic locking

### Collection Response (Array of Categories)

Endpoints that return multiple resources (like `GET /api/categories`) return a **JSON array** at the top level:

```json
[
  {
    "id": 1,
    "name": "Electronics",
    "description": "Gadgets, devices, and accessories",
    "created_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z",
    "updated_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z"
  },
  {
    "id": 2,
    "name": "Books",
    "description": null,
    "created_at": "2024-01-16T11:00:00.000000Z",
    "updated_at": "2024-01-16T11:00:00.000000Z"
  }
]
```

### Single Resource Response (One Category)

Endpoints that create or return a single resource (like `POST /api/categories`) return a **JSON object** at the top level:

```json
{
  "id": 3,
  "name": "Clothing",
  "description": "Apparel and fashion items",
  "created_at": "2024-03-01T08:00:00.000000Z",
  "updated_at": "2024-03-01T08:00:00.000000Z"
}
```

### Empty Collection Response

```json
[]
```

An empty array is a valid, successful response from `GET /api/categories`. It means the database has no category records yet. The HTTP status code will still be `200 OK`.

---

## HTTP Status Codes

This API uses standard HTTP status codes to communicate the outcome of every request. Always check the status code before reading the response body.

### 200 OK

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `200` |
| **Meaning** | The request was received, understood, and processed successfully |
| **Used by** | `GET` requests |
| **Response body** | JSON array of category objects (or empty array `[]`) |

You will receive `200 OK` when you call `GET /api/categories` and the server successfully queries the database.

**Example:**
```json
[
  { "id": 1, "name": "Electronics", "description": null, "created_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z", "updated_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z" }
]
```

**What to do:** Read the response body and use the data.

---

### 201 Created

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `201` |
| **Meaning** | A new resource was successfully created |
| **Used by** | `POST` requests |
| **Response body** | The newly created category object, including its auto-assigned `id` and timestamps |

You will receive `201 Created` when you call `POST /api/categories` with a valid request body.

**Example:**
```json
{
  "id": 5,
  "name": "Sports",
  "description": "Outdoor and fitness equipment",
  "created_at": "2024-06-01T12:00:00.000000Z",
  "updated_at": "2024-06-01T12:00:00.000000Z"
}
```

**What to do:** Store the returned `id` if you need to reference this resource in future requests.

---

### 404 Not Found

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `404` |
| **Meaning** | The requested URL does not match any route registered in Laravel |
| **Used by** | Any request with a wrong URL or a missing resource ID |
| **Response body** | JSON error object (if `Accept: application/json` is set) or HTML page |

You will receive `404 Not Found` when:
- You mistype the URL (e.g., `/api/category` instead of `/api/categories`)
- You request a resource by ID that does not exist (e.g., `GET /api/categories/9999`)
- The route is not registered in `routes/api.php`

**Example response body (with `Accept: application/json`):**
```json
{
  "message": "Not Found"
}
```

**What to do:**
1. Double-check the URL path for typos.
2. Run `php artisan route:list` in your terminal to see all registered routes.
3. If you recently added a new route, clear the route cache: `php artisan route:clear`.

---

### 422 Unprocessable Entity

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `422` |
| **Meaning** | The request was well-formed but failed Laravel's validation rules |
| **Used by** | `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH` requests with invalid or missing fields |
| **Response body** | JSON object with a `message` summary and an `errors` object containing field-level error arrays |

You will receive `422 Unprocessable Entity` when:
- The `name` field is missing from the request body
- The `name` field is empty or not a string
- Any other validation rule defined in the controller is violated

**Example response body (with `Accept: application/json`):**
```json
{
  "message": "The name field is required.",
  "errors": {
    "name": [
      "The name field is required."
    ]
  }
}
```

**What to do:**
1. Read the `errors` object тАФ each key is a field name, and the value is an array of error messages for that field.
2. Fix the request body to include all required fields with valid values.
3. Ensure `Content-Type: application/json` is set so Laravel parses the body correctly.

---

### 500 Internal Server Error

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `500` |
| **Meaning** | The server encountered an unexpected condition that prevented it from fulfilling the request |
| **Used by** | Any request when a server-side exception occurs |
| **Response body** | JSON error object (if `Accept: application/json` is set) or HTML debug page |

You will receive `500 Internal Server Error` when:
- The database is not running or the connection credentials in `.env` are wrong
- There is a PHP exception in the controller or service layer
- The database schema is out of sync with the Eloquent model (run `php artisan migrate`)
- A required Laravel service is misconfigured

**Example response body (with `Accept: application/json`):**
```json
{
  "message": "Server Error"
}
```

In `APP_DEBUG=true` mode (local development), the response will include a full stack trace:
```json
{
  "message": "SQLSTATE[HY000] [2002] Connection refused",
  "exception": "Illuminate\\Database\\QueryException",
  "file": "/var/www/app/vendor/laravel/framework/src/Illuminate/Database/Connection.php",
  "line": 760,
  "trace": [...]
}
```

**What to do:**
1. Check that your database server is running.
2. Verify `DB_HOST`, `DB_PORT`, `DB_DATABASE`, `DB_USERNAME`, `DB_PASSWORD` in your `.env` file.
3. Run pending migrations: `php artisan migrate`.
4. Check the Laravel log file: `tail -f storage/logs/laravel.log`.
5. Open the Laravel Telescope dashboard (if installed) for a full request/exception trace.
6. Set `APP_DEBUG=true` in `.env` locally to get detailed error messages in the response.

---

## Usage Guidelines

### 1. Start the Laravel Development Server

Before sending any request, make sure the Laravel server is running:

```bash
# Using the Artisan serve command (recommended for local development)
php artisan serve

# This starts the server at http://127.0.0.1:8000 by default
# To use a different port:
php artisan serve --port=8080
```

The server must be running at `http://127.0.0.1:8000` for all requests in this collection to work.

### 2. Always Include the Accept Header

Every request must include `Accept: application/json`. This ensures:
- All responses (including errors) are returned as JSON
- You can programmatically parse both success and error responses
- Validation errors (`422`) include field-level messages in the `errors` object
- You avoid receiving Laravel's HTML error pages in your Postman response viewer

### 3. Use a Postman Environment for the Base URL

Instead of hardcoding `http://127.0.0.1:8000` in every request:
1. Create a Postman environment called `Laravel Local`.
2. Add a variable: `base_url` = `http://127.0.0.1:8000`.
3. Select the environment from the top-right dropdown.
4. Use `{{base_url}}/api/categories` in all request URLs.

This makes it trivial to switch between local, staging, and production by changing the active environment.

### 4. Understand the Status Code Before Reading the Body

Always check the HTTP status code in Postman's response panel:
- `200` тЖТ success (GET), read the array
- `201` тЖТ success (POST), read the created object and save the `id`
- `404` тЖТ wrong URL, fix the path
- `422` тЖТ validation failed, read the `errors` object and fix the request body
- `500` тЖТ server-side error, check logs

### 5. Check Laravel Logs for Errors

When you receive a `500` error, the most useful debugging tool is the Laravel log file:

```bash
tail -f storage/logs/laravel.log
```

This streams new log entries in real time as you send requests from Postman.

### 6. Database Setup

If you are running this API for the first time, set up the database:

```bash
# Create the database (if using SQLite, this creates the file automatically)
php artisan migrate

# Optionally seed the database with sample data
php artisan db:seed
```

Without running migrations, all endpoints that query the database will return `500 Internal Server Error` because the tables do not exist.

### 7. Environment Configuration

Make sure your `.env` file is correctly configured:

```env
APP_ENV=local
APP_DEBUG=true
APP_URL=http://127.0.0.1:8000

DB_CONNECTION=mysql
DB_HOST=127.0.0.1
DB_PORT=3306
DB_DATABASE=laravel
DB_USERNAME=root
DB_PASSWORD=
```

After changing `.env`, clear the config cache:

```bash
php artisan config:clear
php artisan cache:clear
```

---

## Getting Started Checklist

- [ ] Laravel development server is running: `php artisan serve`
- [ ] Database is running and `.env` credentials are correct
- [ ] Migrations have been applied: `php artisan migrate`
- [ ] Postman environment `Laravel Local` is created with `base_url = http://127.0.0.1:8000`
- [ ] All requests include the `Accept: application/json` header
- [ ] POST requests include `Content-Type: application/json` and a valid JSON body
- [ ] You have sent `GET /api/categories` and received a `200 OK` response

request.description:
# Get Categories

Retrieves a complete list of all category resources stored in the database. This is a **read-only** endpoint тАФ it does not create, modify, or delete any data. The response is a JSON array where each element represents one category object.

---

## Endpoint

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Method** | `GET` |
| **URL** | `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories` |
| **Authentication** | None required |
| **Request Body** | None |
| **Idempotent** | Yes тАФ calling this endpoint multiple times always returns the same result without side effects |
| **Safe** | Yes тАФ this endpoint never modifies server state |

---

## Required Request Headers

| Header | Value | Required | Purpose |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `Accept` | `application/json` | **Yes** | Instructs Laravel to return all responses (including errors) as JSON rather than HTML pages |

**How to add this header in Postman:**
1. Open this request.
2. Click the **Headers** tab.
3. Add a new row: Key = `Accept`, Value = `application/json`.
4. Ensure the checkbox on the left is **checked** (enabled).
5. Click **Send**.

> `Content-Type` is **not** required for this request because `GET` requests do not have a request body.

---

## Query Parameters

This endpoint does not accept any query parameters. The full list of categories is always returned. Filtering, sorting, and pagination are not currently implemented.

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| *(none)* | тАФ | тАФ | No query parameters are supported |

---

## JSON Response Structure

A successful `200 OK` response returns a **JSON array** at the top level. Each element of the array is a **category object**.

### Category Object Fields

| Field | Type | Nullable | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `id` | `integer` | No | Unique numeric identifier. Auto-assigned by the database. Immutable. |
| `name` | `string` | No | Human-readable display name of the category. UTF-8 encoded. |
| `description` | `string` | Yes | Optional longer description. Will be `null` if not set. |
| `created_at` | `string` (ISO 8601) | No | UTC timestamp of when the record was created. Set automatically by Laravel. |
| `updated_at` | `string` (ISO 8601) | No | UTC timestamp of the last update. Equals `created_at` if never updated. |

### `id` тАФ Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** Positive integer (e.g., `1`, `7`, `42`)
- **Source:** Auto-incremented primary key assigned by the database at creation time
- **Immutable:** Yes тАФ never changes after the record is created
- **Unique:** Guaranteed unique across all category records
- **Never zero or negative** in a real database
- **Primary use:** Reference this value when building URLs for single-resource endpoints:
  ```
  GET    /api/categories/1
  PUT    /api/categories/1
  DELETE /api/categories/1
  ```
- **In your front-end:** Store this as the key in dropdowns, lists, and state management

### `name` тАФ Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTF-8 string
- **Source:** Provided by the client when the category was created
- **Required at creation:** Yes
- **Mutable:** Yes тАФ can be changed via `PUT` or `PATCH`
- **May contain:** Spaces, punctuation, accented characters, Unicode (e.g., `"├Йlectronique"`, `"B├╝cher"`, `"╨Ъ╨░╤В╨╡╨│╨╛╤А╤Ц╤П"`)
- **Primary use:** Display to end users in navigation menus, filter dropdowns, product listings

### `description` тАФ Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTF-8 string or `null`
- **Source:** Optionally provided by the client at creation or update time
- **Required at creation:** No тАФ will be `null` if omitted
- **Mutable:** Yes
- **May contain:** Long-form text, line breaks, Unicode
- **Null handling:** Always check for `null` before rendering:
  ```javascript
  const desc = category.description ?? 'No description provided.';
  ```

### `created_at` тАФ Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTC timestamp string, ISO 8601 format
- **Example:** `"2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z"`
- **Source:** Set automatically by Laravel's Eloquent ORM at creation. Cannot be set manually.
- **Immutable:** Yes
- **Parsing in JavaScript:**
  ```javascript
  const date = new Date(category.created_at);
  console.log(date.toLocaleDateString()); // e.g. "1/15/2024"
  ```

### `updated_at` тАФ Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTC timestamp string, ISO 8601 format
- **Example:** `"2024-03-22T14:45:00.000000Z"`
- **Source:** Updated automatically by Laravel every time the record is saved
- **Equals `created_at`** when the record has never been modified

### Full Response Example (with data)

```json
[
  {
    "id": 1,
    "name": "Electronics",
    "description": "Gadgets, devices, and accessories",
    "created_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z",
    "updated_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z"
  },
  {
    "id": 2,
    "name": "Books",
    "description": null,
    "created_at": "2024-01-16T11:00:00.000000Z",
    "updated_at": "2024-01-16T11:00:00.000000Z"
  },
  {
    "id": 3,
    "name": "Clothing",
    "description": "Apparel and fashion items",
    "created_at": "2024-01-17T08:45:00.000000Z",
    "updated_at": "2024-02-01T10:00:00.000000Z"
  }
]
```

### Empty Response Example

```json
[]
```

An empty array is a **valid, successful response**. It means the `categories` table exists but contains no rows. The HTTP status code will still be `200 OK`. Do not treat this as an error.

---

## HTTP Status Codes

### 200 OK

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `200` |
| **Meaning** | Request succeeded. The server queried the database and returned the result. |
| **When you see it** | The Laravel server is running, the database is reachable, and the route is registered |

**Example response:**
```json
[
  {
    "id": 1,
    "name": "Electronics",
    "description": "Gadgets and devices",
    "created_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z",
    "updated_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z"
  }
]
```

**What to do:** Read the array and use the data. If the array is empty (`[]`), display an empty state in your UI тАФ this is still a success.

---

### 404 Not Found

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `404` |
| **Meaning** | The URL you requested does not match any route registered in Laravel |
| **When you see it** | URL typo, wrong path, or route not registered in `routes/api.php` |

**Common causes for this endpoint:**
- Typing `/api/category` instead of `/api/categories` (missing the `s`)
- Typing `/api/Categories` (wrong case тАФ URLs are case-sensitive)
- The route is missing from `routes/api.php`
- The route cache is stale after a code change

**Example response body (with `Accept: application/json`):**
```json
{
  "message": "Not Found"
}
```

**How to fix:**
1. Verify the exact URL: `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories`
2. List all registered routes:
   ```bash
   php artisan route:list
   ```
3. Clear the route cache:
   ```bash
   php artisan route:clear
   ```

---

### 500 Internal Server Error

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `500` |
| **Meaning** | The server encountered an unexpected error while processing the request |
| **When you see it** | Database connection failure, PHP exception in the controller, missing table |

**Common causes for this endpoint:**
- The database server is not running
- `DB_HOST`, `DB_PORT`, `DB_DATABASE`, `DB_USERNAME`, or `DB_PASSWORD` in `.env` is incorrect
- The `categories` table does not exist (migrations not run)
- A PHP exception is thrown inside the controller

**Example response body (with `Accept: application/json`, `APP_DEBUG=true`):**
```json
{
  "message": "SQLSTATE[HY000] [2002] Connection refused",
  "exception": "Illuminate\\Database\\QueryException",
  "file": "/var/www/app/vendor/laravel/framework/src/Illuminate/Database/Connection.php",
  "line": 760,
  "trace": []
}
```

**How to fix:**
1. Confirm your database server is running.
2. Check your `.env` database credentials.
3. Run pending migrations:
   ```bash
   php artisan migrate
   ```
4. Tail the Laravel log file:
   ```bash
   tail -f storage/logs/laravel.log
   ```
5. Set `APP_DEBUG=true` in `.env` to get a full stack trace in the response.

---

## Usage Guidelines

### Sending the Request from JavaScript (fetch)

```javascript
const response = await fetch('http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories', {
  method: 'GET',
  headers: {
    'Accept': 'application/json'
  }
});

if (!response.ok) {
  throw new Error(`HTTP error: ${response.status}`);
}

const categories = await response.json();
console.log(categories);
// Output: [{ id: 1, name: 'Electronics', description: '...', created_at: '...', updated_at: '...' }, ...]
```

### Using the `id` Field in Subsequent Requests

The `id` returned by this endpoint is the key you use to interact with individual categories:

```
GET    /api/categories/1      тЖТ fetch a single category
PUT    /api/categories/1      тЖТ update a category
DELETE /api/categories/1      тЖТ delete a category
```

Never hardcode IDs тАФ always use the values returned by the API, as they differ between development, staging, and production databases.

### Handling a Null `description`

```javascript
categories.forEach(category => {
  const desc = category.description ?? 'No description available.';
  console.log(`${category.name}: ${desc}`);
});
```

### Handling an Empty Array

```javascript
if (categories.length === 0) {
  console.log('No categories yet. Use POST /api/categories to create one.');
} else {
  // render the list
}
```

### Postman Test Scripts

Add these to the **Scripts тЖТ Post-response** tab to automatically validate the response:

```javascript
pm.test('Status code is 200', function () {
  pm.response.to.have.status(200);
});

pm.test('Response is a JSON array', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  pm.expect(body).to.be.an('array');
});

pm.test('Each category has id, name, created_at, updated_at', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  body.forEach(function (category) {
    pm.expect(category).to.have.property('id').that.is.a('number');
    pm.expect(category).to.have.property('name').that.is.a('string');
    pm.expect(category).to.have.property('created_at').that.is.a('string');
    pm.expect(category).to.have.property('updated_at').that.is.a('string');
  });
});

pm.test('description is string or null', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  body.forEach(function (category) {
    const desc = category.description;
    pm.expect(desc === null || typeof desc === 'string').to.be.true;
  });
});
```

---

## Prerequisites Checklist

- [ ] Laravel server is running: `php artisan serve`
- [ ] Database server is running and accessible
- [ ] `.env` database credentials are correct
- [ ] Migrations have been applied: `php artisan migrate`
- [ ] `Accept: application/json` header is present and enabled in the Headers tab
- [ ] Correct Postman environment is selected (if using `{{base_url}}`)

# Post Categories

Creates a new category resource in the database. This is a **write** endpoint тАФ it inserts a new record and returns the full created object, including the auto-assigned `id` and timestamps. Sending this request multiple times with the same body will create multiple separate category records (it is **not** idempotent).

---

## Endpoint

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Method** | `POST` |
| **URL** | `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories` |
| **Authentication** | None required |
| **Request Body** | JSON object with category fields |
| **Idempotent** | No тАФ each call creates a new record |
| **Safe** | No тАФ this endpoint modifies server state |

---

## Required Request Headers

| Header | Value | Required | Purpose |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `Accept` | `application/json` | **Yes** | Instructs Laravel to return all responses (including validation errors) as JSON rather than HTML pages |
| `Content-Type` | `application/json` | **Yes** | Tells Laravel to parse the request body as JSON. Without this, `$request->input('name')` returns `null` and validation fails. |

**How to add these headers in Postman:**
1. Open this request.
2. Click the **Headers** tab.
3. Add two rows:
   - Key = `Accept`, Value = `application/json`
   - Key = `Content-Type`, Value = `application/json`
4. Ensure both checkboxes are **checked** (enabled).

> **Shortcut:** If you select **Body тЖТ raw тЖТ JSON** in Postman, the `Content-Type: application/json` header is added automatically. You still need to add `Accept: application/json` manually.

---

## Request Body

The request body must be a **JSON object**. Send it in the **Body тЖТ raw тЖТ JSON** tab in Postman.

### Request Body Fields

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `name` | `string` | **Yes** | The display name of the new category. Must be a non-empty string. |
| `description` | `string` | No | An optional longer description of the category. Omit this field or set it to `null` if not needed. |

### Minimal Request Body (name only)

```json
{
  "name": "Electronics"
}
```

### Full Request Body (name and description)

```json
{
  "name": "Electronics",
  "description": "Gadgets, devices, and accessories including phones, laptops, and cameras."
}
```

### Field Rules

#### `name`
- **Required:** Yes. Omitting it or sending an empty string will result in a `422 Unprocessable Entity` response.
- **Type:** String. Sending a number or boolean will fail validation.
- **Max length:** Depends on the database column definition (typically 255 characters for a `VARCHAR(255)`).
- **Uniqueness:** Not enforced at the API level unless the database has a unique constraint.
- **Examples of valid values:** `"Books"`, `"Home & Garden"`, `"├Йlectronique"`, `"B├╝cher"`
- **Examples of invalid values:** `""` (empty string), `null`, `123` (number), omitted entirely

#### `description`
- **Required:** No. If omitted, the field will be stored as `null` in the database and returned as `null` in the response.
- **Type:** String or `null`.
- **Max length:** Typically unlimited (stored as `TEXT` in the database).
- **May contain:** Long-form text, line breaks, Unicode characters.

---

## JSON Response Structure

A successful `201 Created` response returns a **JSON object** representing the newly created category.

### Response Fields

| Field | Type | Nullable | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `id` | `integer` | No | The unique numeric identifier assigned by the database. Use this in future requests. |
| `name` | `string` | No | The name you provided in the request body. |
| `description` | `string` | Yes | The description you provided, or `null` if not provided. |
| `created_at` | `string` (ISO 8601) | No | UTC timestamp of when the record was created. Set automatically by Laravel. |
| `updated_at` | `string` (ISO 8601) | No | UTC timestamp of the last update. Equals `created_at` immediately after creation. |

### `id` тАФ Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** Positive integer (e.g., `1`, `7`, `42`)
- **Source:** Auto-incremented primary key assigned by the database at the moment of insertion
- **Immutable:** Yes тАФ never changes after creation
- **Unique:** Guaranteed unique across all category records
- **Critical:** Save this value immediately after a successful `POST`. You will need it to update or delete this category:
  ```
  PUT    /api/categories/{id}
  DELETE /api/categories/{id}
  ```
- **Do not assume sequential IDs** тАФ if records have been deleted, there may be gaps (e.g., `1, 2, 5, 6`)

### `name` тАФ Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTF-8 string
- **Value:** Exactly what you sent in the request body (after Laravel trims whitespace, if configured)
- **Mutable:** Yes тАФ can be changed via `PUT` or `PATCH` endpoints
- **Primary use:** Display to end users in navigation menus, filter dropdowns, product listings

### `description` тАФ Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTF-8 string or `null`
- **Value:** What you sent in the request body, or `null` if you omitted the field
- **Mutable:** Yes тАФ can be set or updated via `PUT` or `PATCH`
- **Null handling:** Always check for `null` before rendering in your UI:
  ```javascript
  const desc = category.description ?? 'No description provided.';
  ```

### `created_at` тАФ Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTC timestamp string, ISO 8601 format
- **Example:** `"2024-06-01T12:00:00.000000Z"`
- **Source:** Set automatically by Laravel's Eloquent ORM at the moment of insertion. You cannot set this manually.
- **Immutable:** Yes
- **Parsing in JavaScript:**
  ```javascript
  const date = new Date(category.created_at);
  console.log(date.toLocaleDateString()); // e.g. "6/1/2024"
  ```

### `updated_at` тАФ Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTC timestamp string, ISO 8601 format
- **Value immediately after creation:** Equals `created_at`
- **Source:** Updated automatically by Laravel every time the record is saved

### Full Response Example (with description)

```json
{
  "id": 5,
  "name": "Electronics",
  "description": "Gadgets, devices, and accessories including phones, laptops, and cameras.",
  "created_at": "2024-06-01T12:00:00.000000Z",
  "updated_at": "2024-06-01T12:00:00.000000Z"
}
```

### Full Response Example (without description)

```json
{
  "id": 6,
  "name": "Books",
  "description": null,
  "created_at": "2024-06-01T12:05:00.000000Z",
  "updated_at": "2024-06-01T12:05:00.000000Z"
}
```

---

## HTTP Status Codes

### 201 Created

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `201` |
| **Meaning** | A new category was successfully created and stored in the database |
| **When you see it** | The request body is valid, the server is running, and the database is reachable |

**Example response:**
```json
{
  "id": 5,
  "name": "Sports",
  "description": "Outdoor and fitness equipment",
  "created_at": "2024-06-01T12:00:00.000000Z",
  "updated_at": "2024-06-01T12:00:00.000000Z"
}
```

**What to do:** Store the returned `id` if you need to reference this category in future requests.

---

### 422 Unprocessable Entity

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `422` |
| **Meaning** | The request was well-formed but failed Laravel's validation rules |
| **When you see it** | `name` is missing, empty, or not a string |

**Example: missing `name` field**

Request body sent:
```json
{}
```

Response:
```json
{
  "message": "The name field is required.",
  "errors": {
    "name": [
      "The name field is required."
    ]
  }
}
```

**Example: empty `name` field**

Request body sent:
```json
{
  "name": ""
}
```

Response:
```json
{
  "message": "The name field is required.",
  "errors": {
    "name": [
      "The name field is required."
    ]
  }
}
```

**How to fix:**
1. Read the `errors` object тАФ each key is a field name, and the value is an array of error messages.
2. Ensure `name` is present and is a non-empty string.
3. Ensure `Content-Type: application/json` is set so Laravel parses the body correctly.
4. Ensure the body is valid JSON (no trailing commas, all strings quoted).

---

### 404 Not Found

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `404` |
| **Meaning** | The URL does not match any route registered in Laravel |
| **When you see it** | URL typo or route not registered in `routes/api.php` |

**Example response body (with `Accept: application/json`):**
```json
{
  "message": "Not Found"
}
```

**How to fix:**
1. Verify the URL: `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories`
2. Check registered routes: `php artisan route:list`
3. Clear route cache: `php artisan route:clear`

---

### 500 Internal Server Error

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `500` |
| **Meaning** | The server encountered an unexpected error while processing the request |
| **When you see it** | Database connection failure, PHP exception, missing table |

**Common causes:**
- The database server is not running
- `.env` database credentials are incorrect
- The `categories` table does not exist (migrations not run)
- A PHP exception is thrown inside the controller

**Example response body (with `APP_DEBUG=true`):**
```json
{
  "message": "SQLSTATE[HY000] [2002] Connection refused",
  "exception": "Illuminate\\Database\\QueryException",
  "file": "/var/www/app/vendor/laravel/framework/src/Illuminate/Database/Connection.php",
  "line": 760,
  "trace": []
}
```

**How to fix:**
1. Confirm your database server is running.
2. Check `.env` database credentials (`DB_HOST`, `DB_PORT`, `DB_DATABASE`, `DB_USERNAME`, `DB_PASSWORD`).
3. Run pending migrations: `php artisan migrate`
4. Tail the log file: `tail -f storage/logs/laravel.log`
5. Set `APP_DEBUG=true` in `.env` to get a full stack trace in the response.

---

## Usage Guidelines

### Sending the Request from JavaScript (fetch)

```javascript
const response = await fetch('http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories', {
  method: 'POST',
  headers: {
    'Accept': 'application/json',
    'Content-Type': 'application/json'
  },
  body: JSON.stringify({
    name: 'Electronics',
    description: 'Gadgets, devices, and accessories'
  })
});

if (response.status === 422) {
  const error = await response.json();
  console.error('Validation failed:', error.errors);
} else if (!response.ok) {
  throw new Error(`HTTP error: ${response.status}`);
} else {
  const category = await response.json();
  console.log('Created category with id:', category.id);
}
```

### Saving the Returned `id`

After a successful `201 Created` response, always save the `id` from the response body:

```javascript
const category = await response.json();
const newCategoryId = category.id;
// Use newCategoryId in future GET, PUT, DELETE requests
```

Do not assume what the `id` will be тАФ always read it from the response.

### Handling Validation Errors

```javascript
if (response.status === 422) {
  const error = await response.json();
  // error.errors is an object: { fieldName: ["error message", ...] }
  Object.entries(error.errors).forEach(([field, messages]) => {
    console.error(`${field}: ${messages.join(', ')}`);
  });
}
```

### Postman Test Scripts

Add these to the **Scripts тЖТ Post-response** tab to automatically validate the response:

```javascript
pm.test('Status code is 201', function () {
  pm.response.to.have.status(201);
});

pm.test('Response is a JSON object', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  pm.expect(body).to.be.an('object').and.not.an('array');
});

pm.test('Response has id (number)', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  pm.expect(body).to.have.property('id').that.is.a('number');
});

pm.test('Response has name (string)', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  pm.expect(body).to.have.property('name').that.is.a('string');
});

pm.test('Response has created_at and updated_at', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  pm.expect(body).to.have.property('created_at').that.is.a('string');
  pm.expect(body).to.have.property('updated_at').that.is.a('string');
});

pm.test('description is string or null', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  const desc = body.description;
  pm.expect(desc === null || typeof desc === 'string').to.be.true;
});

// Save the created id as an environment variable for use in other requests
const body = pm.response.json();
if (body.id) {
  pm.environment.set('category_id', body.id);
  console.log('Saved category_id:', body.id);
}
```

- [ ] Database server is running and accessible

- [ ] `Accept: application/json` header is present and enabled
- [ ] `Content-Type: application/json` header is present and enabled
- [ ] Request body is valid JSON with a non-empty `name` field
- [ ] Correct Postman environment is selected (if using `{{base_url}}`)
